name: Extispicy
shortDescription: Extispicy was a form of divination practiced in ancient Mesopotamia, primarily involving the examination of the entrails of sacrificed animals, especially the liver, to discern the will of the gods.
description: Extispicy, also known as hepatoscopy, was a crucial form of divination in ancient Babylonian religion. It involved the meticulous examination of the internal organs, most notably the liver (hence hepatoscopy), of sacrificed animals, typically sheep. Diviners, known as baru priests, believed that the gods inscribed their will onto these organs, providing insights into future events, the success of undertakings, or the causes of misfortune. The shape, size, color, and markings of the liver, gall bladder, intestines, and other organs were carefully scrutinized and compared to established models and omens documented in extensive cuneiform texts. These texts, such as the liver model texts, provided a framework for interpreting the observed features. A malformed or unusually marked organ could signify impending disaster, while a healthy and 'normal' organ indicated divine favor. The practice was complex and required years of training to master the intricate system of correspondences. Extispicy was not merely a superstitious practice; it was a highly formalized and systematized approach to understanding the divine realm and influencing human affairs. The results of extispicy could influence decisions regarding warfare, governance, and personal matters. The belief underlying extispicy was that the gods were intimately involved in the world and communicated their intentions through observable phenomena, including the anatomy of sacrificial animals. Shamash, the sun god and god of justice, was particularly associated with divination, as was Marduk, the chief god of Babylon, and Ea, the god of wisdom and magic. While related to other forms of divination like astrology and omen-interpretation, extispicy held a central role in Babylonian religious and political life.

sources.title: Divination and Interpretation of Signs in the Ancient World
sources.author: Leick, Gwendolyn
